summary: Stancil argues that consumer products like Yelp made data-driven decisions feel natural by embedding data inside the experience, while corporate analytics isolates data in dashboards divorced from work. The future of operational analytics is to integrate focused, disciplined data experiences directly into workflows and build a data “operating system” that enables those experiences rather than just more BI tooling.
money_quotes: We just did it, all of us, on our own.

These services don’t attempt to make you “data-driven.” Instead, they focus on a bigger problem—choosing a restaurant, a means of transit, and a reservation booking—and integrate data alongside other features, like phone numbers, pictures of food, and links to menu, that help you address it.
Dashboards are the opposite. They’re often data, looking for a problem.
Our ambition shouldn’t be to make decisions automatically.
The future is an experience, with data integrated into and inseparable from how we do our jobs.
key_insight: Operational analytics will only become ubiquitous when data is embedded seamlessly into the tools and decisions people already use, not isolated in standalone dashboards.
